coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent conjugated microporous polymer (CMP) derived sensor array for multiple Organic/Inorganic contaminants detection
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Ruru
Zhao, Wei
Qiu, Qian
Xie, Aming
Cheng, Siyao
Jiao, Yingzhi
Pan, Xihao
Dong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The development of fluorescent sensors for highly sensitive detection of multiple environmental contaminants is highly promising, but still remains to be a challenge up to date. In this work, we provide a highly water-dispersible sulfonated conjugated microporous polymers (S-CMP) based fluorescent sensor array that can be used for high-throughput detection of multiple organic/inorganic contaminants. This S-CMP was synthesized via a two-step process, including a Pd-catalyzed coupling reaction and a sulfonation process. It is differentiated twelve metal ions using principal component analysis (PCA) on the basis of distinguished fluorescent responses of the sensor array on metal ions. Moreover, it is also identified a series of or;1;ganic solvents by this S-CMP-based sensor by virtue of its unique fluorescence-emitting and color response. This work opens up the design of sulfonate-grafted CMP in environmental pollution detection and expands the application scope of CMP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfonated conjugated microporous polymers
Ion recognition
Fluorescent sensor array
Solvatochromic effect
Organics detection

Luminescence properties of CMP and S-CMP
Sensor array for distinguishing metal ions based on S-CMP
Solvatochromic behavior of S-CMP for sensing small organic molecules

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-B) HR-TEM images of S-CMP. (C–E) Element mapping analysis of S-CMP.
(A) FTIR spectra of the CMP before and after sulfonation. (B) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm of CMP and S-CMP at 77 K.
(A) Survey scan of XPS data for S-CMP. (B-D) High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and S 2p of S-CMP.

(A) The excitation spectra of S-CMP. (B) The emission spectra of S-CMP under excitation at 470 nm (black line) and emission spectra of S-CMP under excitation at 365 nm (red line) of S-CMP. (C) The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram. (D) Simplified schematic diagrams of the red shift of emission wavelength after sulfonation on CMP (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(A) The emission spectra of S-CMP in different pH aqueous solutions (λem =365 nm). (B) The corresponding photographs irradiated under a UV lamp (top) and visible light (bottom).

(A) The corresponding photographs of S-CMP irradiated under a UV lamp (λex =365 nm) (top) and visible light (bottom) after addition of different metal ions at neutral condition. (B) The fluorescent response patterns (F470/F557) of the S-CMP sensor array toward 12 metal ions (100 μM). The error bars represent standard deviations of three parallel measurements. (C) The heat map originated from fluorescent response patterns of the sensor array towards different metal ions. (D) Three-dimensional PCA plot of the fluorescent responses obtained from the S-CMP sensor array toward 12 metal ions.

(A) Solvent response observed for S-CMP under a UV lamp (λex =365 nm) (top) and visible light (bottom). (B) Fluorescent spectra of S-CMP immersed in the eleven organic solvents. (C) A 2D decoded map of S-CMP based on the F455 and F557 responses toward the eleven organic molecules.
Schematic representation of the sulfonated microporous polymer (S-CMP) and its multi-target sensing.
Comparison with the published sensor arrays for metal ions recognition.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: concentrations of metal ions in sensor arrays.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent conjugated microporous polymer (CMP) derived sensor array for multiple Organic/Inorganic contaminants detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of fluorescent sensors for highly sensitive detection of multiple environmental contaminants is highly promising, but still remains to be a challenge up to date. In this work, we provide a highly water-dispersible sulfonated conjugated microporous polymers (S-CMP) based fluorescent sensor array that can be used for high-throughput detection of multiple organic/inorganic contaminants. This S-CMP was synthesized via a two-step process, including a Pd-catalyzed coupling reaction and a sulfonation process. It is differentiated twelve metal ions using principal component analysis (PCA) on the basis of distinguished fluorescent responses of the sensor array on metal ions. Moreover, it is also identified a series of or;1;ganic solvents by this S-CMP-based sensor by virtue of its unique fluorescence-emitting and color response. This work opens up the design of sulfonate-grafted CMP in environmental pollution detection and expands the application scope of CMP.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollutants, especially heavy metal ions and organic wastes, are increasing concerned because of their serious hazards toward human beings [1–4]. It is of considerable importance to identify and monitor these hazardous chemicals before further environmental governance. However, the developing of an efficient sensor that can response and discriminate diverse organic wastes solvents is still a challenging task [5–7]. On the other hand, there is still lack of sensors for efficient recognition of multiple metal ions, which is because traditional methods can’t detect multiple analytes in one sample owing to their “lock and key” identification mode [8–12]. To address these issues, it is believed that the development of multi-analyte sensors that can simultaneously monitor multiple organic/inorganic contaminants is highly desirable.

Conjugated microporous polymers (CMPs), π-conjugate polymer skeletons with tunable porosity, electrical and optical properties [13–20], have recently demonstrated excellent performance in various fields, such as heterogeneous catalysis [21–23], energy storage [24,25], gas capture and storage [26,27]. CMPs containing fluorophores on the skeleton benefit exciton migration over the whole framework, which allows their luminescent properties [28]. Compared to conventional conjugated polymers, chain aggregation will be restrained by the unique micropores, and thus suppress the dissipation of excitation energy and ultimately improve the light-emitting properties [29,30]. Those CMPs with intense luminescent properties and visible emission colors may be good candidates as optical sensing materials for pollutants detection, by virtue of their π-π stacking, polarization and H-bonding induced discriminative absorption and emission behavior [31–35]. However, the application of CMPs as sensor array for organic pollution and metal ions optical detection still remains to be unexplored, despite of some splendid reports on fluorescent properties of several CMPs [36–39].

In this work, we provide a highly water-dispersible sulfonated (S)-CMP based fluorescent sensor array that can be used for highly sensitive multiple organic/inorganic contaminants detection. Firstly, we synthesized the S-CMP via a two-step process referring a Pd-catalyzed coupling of 2,4,6-tris(4-bromophenyl)-1,3,5-triazine with 1,4-phenylenediboronic acid, and a sulfonation by HSO3Cl (Scheme 1 ). It is found that the introduction of sulfonic groups exerts obvious influence on the luminescent properties of S-CMP, comparing to that without sulfonation. Then, we systematically determined the fluorescence responses of S-CMP toward different metal ions, and constructed an orthogonal sensor array that exhibits obviously discriminative responses toward numerous metal ions. Finally, solvent effect on ﬂuorescence properties of S-CMP were investigated. It is indicated that S-CMP sensor can also be applied as an “Artificial Nose” to distinguish a series of organic solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CMP and S-CMP
ce:section.ce:para: S-CMP was prepared by a two-step synthesis process (Scheme 1). Firstly, a CMP was synthesized through Pd-catalyzed Suzuki-Miyaura coupling reaction of 1,4-phenylenediboronic acid and 2,4,6-tris (4-bromophenyl)-1,3,5-triazine. Then, the CMP was sulfonated by HSO3Cl and furnished with sulfonic acid groups (S-CMP). The morphology of S-CMP and CMP were characterized through scanning electron microscope (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). As is observed in Figs. 1 A and S1 that both CMP and S-CMP appear as irregular nanoparticles in nano scale. When the scale bar is 5 nm (Fig. 1B), HR-TEM image exhibit alternating dark and bright area, probably indicating the presence of micropore [36]. The element mapping analysis further indicates the uniformly distribution of sulfur in S-CMP support (Fig. 1C-E).

The changes in the structure of CMP before and after sulfonation treatment was confirmed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis (Fig. 2 A). The sharp peak at 807 cm−1 belongs to the out-of-plane bending vibration of p-substituted phenyl in CMP skeleton. The appearance of vibrational bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1506 cm−1 are assigned to triazine ring mode. After treatment with chlorosulfonic acid, a new peak at 1167 cm−1 could be assigned to the O = SO asymmetric stretching modes, together with the absorption at 3378 cm−1 corresponding to −OH stretching vibration, thus confirming the presence of -SO3H groups in S-CMP. The Zeta-potential of S-CMP is −22.1 mV at pH 7.4, indicating a negative surface charge (Figure S2). For CMP, the Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET) surface area (SBET) and pore volume (Vpore) were calculated to be 192.217 m²/g and 0.106 cm³/g, respectively. Compared with CMP, the SBET and Vpore plunge to 36.619 m²/g and 0.078 cm³/g for S-CMP, which is probably attributed to pore blocking by introduction of sulfonic acid groups on the frame of CMP. This assumption can be further explained by nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis at 77 K (Fig. 2B), which shows a clear decreased nitrogen adsorption isotherm for S-CMP. The pore-size distribution calculated by nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT) is provided in Fig. S3. It is observed that CMP shows strong peaks at 4.0, and lower than 2 nm (Fig. S3A), indicating that CMP belongs to microporous polymer. After sulfonation, the volume of micropores decrease sharply, evidenced by Fig. S3B. This phenomenon may be caused by the destruction of polymer skeleton under sulfonation process, or by the introduction of sulfonic acid group that hamper N2 to get into S-CMP during nitrogen adsorption and desorption measurement.

Detailed information about the sulfur atoms incorporated in S-CMP was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Figs. 3 A, both S2s and S2p peaks were observed in the survey spectra of S-CMP, indicating the successful incorporation of sulfur into the framework through sulfonation treatment. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p are shown in Fig. 3B-D. The C 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 286.29 eV and 284.53 eV, representing CN/C-N/CS bonds and CC/C-C bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectra can be divided to two peaks at 401.09 eV and 398.57 eV, which are attributed to =NH+- and CN/C-N, respectively. The S 2p spectra have only one peak (168.44 eV) corresponding to the binding energies of -SO3H groups, which indicates -SO3H is the main form incorporated S-CMP. The relative amount of − SO3H on the surface of S-CMP can be calculated roughly to be 71.9 : 7.1 : 3.4 (C/N/S), which is very close to the C/N ratio in CMP (10:1). It is calculated that one ring unit (Red pattern in Scheme 1) averagely corresponds to 2.8 -SO3H groups, suggesting a high sulfonation rate in prepared S-CMP. In addition, a titration experiment was carried out to measure the content of -SO3H in S-CMP. The amount of -SO3H per ring unit was determined to be 3.2, which is approximate to that measured by XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence properties of CMP and S-CMP
ce:section.ce:para:
Photoluminescence studies in aqueous solution were performed on S-CMP and CMP without postsulfonation as a control material. The introduction of sulfonic acid groups influences both the structure of CMP and their fluorescence properties greatly. With exactly the same skeleton but without postsulfonation, CMP shows fluorescence emission at 455 nm. The quantum yields (ψ) in this work were calculated by eqn (1) as follows:
(1)
ψ =
ψ R
F
X − int e r g a l
F
R − int e r g a l
A R
A X
n x 2
n R 2

In eqn (1), rhodamine 6 G was used as the reference compound for test. The UV–vis absorbance intensities of samples and reference compound were controlled less than 1. “FX-integral” and “FR-integral” are the integral area of fluorescence spectra for samples and reference compound, respectively. “AX” and “AR” are the UV–vis absorbance intensities. “nX” and “nR” are the refractive index of the solvents. The quantum yield of rhodamine 6 G (ψR) dissolved in ethanol is 0.95. According to eqn (1), ψCMP is determined as 1.9 %. The CIE coordinates are determined as X = 0.1464, Y = 0.1333, which is located in blue region (Fig. S4). Furthermore, when the emission at 455 nm was monitored, CMP displayed a sharp excitation band at around 384 nm (Fig. S4A). Upon modifying the CMP with sulfonation, S-CMP shows distinct luminescent phenomenon (Fig. 4 ). The excitation spectrum of S-CMP, monitored at an emission wavelength of 557 nm, exhibits two obvious bands with maximum absorption at 365 nm and 470 nm, respectively (Fig. 4A). The changes of excitation wavelength between CMP and S-CMP indicate a new fluorescence chromophore produced trough sulfonation, which can be further verified by UV–vis spectra (Figure S5). Under excitation at 470 nm, S-CMP has sharp fluorescent emission centered at 557 nm (Fig. 4B). The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram were calculated to be (0.3609, 0.4981), which are located in green-yellow light-emission (Fig. 4C). Exciting S-CMP at 365 nm yields a broad band in the range of 400–600 nm with CIE coordinates (0.2497, 0.3668) (Fig. 4B-C). More importantly, the quantum yield of S-CMP (ψS-CMP) increased to be 7.4 %, implying it can serve as an excellent candidate for luminescent sensors. We attribute this fluorescent effect on CMP to the introduction of sulfonic acid groups, which can extend their delocalization of π-electron systems in CMP. Meanwhile, functionalization with -SO3H induces the increased π* energy level of the ligand, consequently, the energy gap between π* and π level of the material is closer, resulting in the red shift of emission wavelength (Fig. 4D).

The stability of CMPs in different chemical environments is important for their sensing applications. We found the luminescent properties of S-CMP showed little changes during 5d storage at room temperature, suggesting its good luminescent stability (Fig. S6). In addition, water solubility is essential for CMPs to be a qualified fluorescent sensor. Different from the poor dispersion of CMP, S-CMP is readily soluble in water owing to the introduction of sulfonic groups, further indicating its possibility as a fluorescent sensor to monitor environment pollutants. Therefore, the stability of S-CMP can be measured in aqueous solutions at different pH values. The pH-sensitive behavior of the S-CMP was investigated in NaOH or HCl aqueous solutions with pH ranging from 2.0–12.0. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of S-CMP almost remains constant with increasing pH from 2.0–10.0, indicating its excellent pH tolerance. However, the emission color changed from green-yellow to blue suddenly when pH increasing to 11.0. We also investigated the recyclable performance of S-CMP between pH = 3.0 and 11.0 (Fig. S7). Three runs were performed by exchange of the two pH solutions. Interestingly, the emission color switched from green to blue regularly and the corresponding fluorescence intensity of S-CMP was well consistent with that of the original one, which suggests that S-CMP can be reversibly “used” and “recovered” as a fluorescent sensor. We speculate the fluorescence sensitivity of S-CMP to basic conditions may result from proton transfer mechanism induced by pH. The proton in the free sulfonic acid group can be captured by OH- under alkaline solutions and the sulfonic acid groups in S-CMP are no longer available to join the electronic delocalization, resulting in the color changes of luminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor array for distinguishing metal ions based on S-CMP
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated above, S-CMP exhibits stable fluorescent emission which benefits its application as a fluorescent sensor. In addition, the triazine structure in S-CMP has open coordination sites for metal ions, which can induce the conformational change of S-CMP, with the change of luminescent properties. For each metal ion, S-CMP shows differentiated fluorescent responses because of the different coordination ability between metal ions and sulfonic acid groups. Moreover, S-CMP displays different fluorescent performances under alkaline conditions, indicating the high-throughput recognition for metal ions may be achieved by a sensor array of pH-involved S-CMP.

To study the discrimination ability of our proposed S-CMP sensor array, the fluorescence responses of the S-CMP towards different metal ions at neutral condition were first determined. Twelve metal ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+ were selected as the representative analytes. Nitrate ion was taken as the counterion for the cations owing to its good solubility. As shown in Fig. 6 A, S-CMP exhibits different fluorescent responses to the twelve metal ions, the corresponding fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. S8. According to the fluorescence spectra of S-CMP after adding various metal ions (Fig. S8), Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+ have obvious fluorescence quenching effect at neutral condition. Other metal ions such as Ag+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+ are more likely to induce blue shift of luminescence. We speculate the main reason of the quenching can be ascribed to the non-emissive interaction between the lone pair electrons of the triazinyl-N atom and unfilled d-orbitals of Fe3+, Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions via the intersystem crossing mechanism [40]. Moreover, the interaction between sulfonic group in S-CMP and Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+ may promote charge transfer and thus constrain excitation recombination, resulting in obvious decrease of fluorescence. The most applied mechanism for Ag+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+ induced blue shift of luminescence is intermolecular charge transfer (ICT), which has been clearly explained by de Silva et al. [41]. If a fluorophore (CMP without sulfonation) is directly connected with an electron acceptor (sulfonic acid group) to form a π-electron conjugation system, ICT between the electron donor and acceptor could be enhanced by light excitation. If a proton acceptor (triazine structure: also called electron donor) interacts with metal cation, it reduces the electron donating ability of the acceptor, resulting in blue shift of the emission wavelength [42]. Compared with alkali and alkaline earth metal ions, Ag+, Zn2+, Cu2+ with stronger electron reception ability are more likely to induce blue shift of luminescence. In all, the S-CMP sensor at neutral condition can identify Zn2+, Ag+ and Fe3+ from the twelve metal ions effectively, however, it can't further differentiate similar metal ions such as different alkali metal ions (Na+, K+), alkaline-earth metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+ and Ba2+), transition/post-transition metal ions (Pb2+, Al3+ and Cr3+). Therefore, a sensor array which can accurately distinguish all the above metal ions is in high demand.

The multiple readouts for the construction of the sensor array originated from the fluorescence values of S-CMP at three pH conditions (3.0, 7.0 and 11.0) and the fluorescence spectra for each metal ion are shown in Fig. S9. The various responses of the sensor array towards metal ions are depicted in Fig. 6B-D. Each metal ion was measured for three replicates, giving data pattern of (F470/F557). The raw data matrix (3 receptors × 12 metal ions × 3 replicates) was used to draw the heat map and further analyzed by principal component analysis (PCA). Fig. 6B suggests that the S-CMP sensor exhibits different fluorescent response mode to the twelve metal ions, which could be effectively distinguished by heat map (Fig. 6C). To further demonstrate the distinguishing capacity of S-CMP as sensor array, PCA was further used to quantitatively differentiate the resultant patterns. PCA is a statistical analysis method, which transforms all data into linearly unrelated components through matrix conversion and puts the most significant factors (variance) into a lower dimensional space. In this work, three principal components (63.33 %, 32.50 % and 4.17 %) were employed to produce a three-dimensional (3D) plot, in which each dot represents the response mode for an individual sample against the S-CMP sensor array. In Fig. 6D, 12 separate dot clusters, corresponding to 12 metal ions, are obviously distinguished in this S-CMP-based recognition system, suggesting the feasibility of the sensor array as a new “chemical nose”. Furthermore, the short distances among the three replicates in every cluster indicate the high accuracy and reproducibility of this sensor array. Compared with the reported sensor arrays in recent years (Table 1 ), our sensor array has advantages such as fast response, more kinds of metal ions, water detection system closer to natural sample, fluorescent and colorimetric dual-response. The sensor arrays reported by Zhang’s group achieved more kinds of metal ions than our method. However, these methods involved DNA or protein which needs expensive cost and severe operating condition, limiting their wider applicability.

The anti-interference performance of the sensor array towards other metal ions was further explored by studying their cross-reactivity. Four metal ions rich in water including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ were chosen as the possible interferences and Pb2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ag+ were the target analytes. The fluorescent responses of the S-CMP sensor array in the presence of the same concentration of interference were recorded and further analyzed by PCA. As shown in Fig. S9, these interferences could also induce array’s responses, however, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ag+ were clearly separated without any overlap. The reproducibility was evaluated by five sensor arrays prepared independently with the same procedure and using Zn2+ as an example. The fluorescent responses obtained from the five parallel sensor arrays towards the detection of 100 mM Zn2+ were recorded in Fig. S10. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the sensor units (S-CMP at pH = 3.0, pH = 7.0 and pH = 11.0, respectively) were calculated to be 1.24 %, 3.33 % and 4.69 %, indicating good reproducibility of this S-CMP based sensor arrays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvatochromic behavior of S-CMP for sensing small organic molecules
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential of S-CMP for sensing organic small molecules, solvent-dependent spectroscopic studies of S-CMP were then investigated. The solid yellow sample was immersed in various solvents, including dichloromethane (DCM), acetone (DMK), N-butane (N-Bu), tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethanol (EtOH), acetonitrile (ACN), benzene (PhH), toluene (Tol), formamid (For), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). As shown in Fig. 7 A and B, the fluorescence properties of S-CMP are highly solvent dependent. In weak polar solvents, S-CMP exhibits intense yellow fluorescence, a maximum emission wavelength at 557 nm. Upon the addition of strong polar solvents such as (For, DMF, and DMSO), either a change in solubility, emission color, or fluorescence intensity was observed. Addition of these solvents converted the initial yellow suspension into milky white suspension. The samples in strong polar solvents were found to give rise to blue emission when excited at 365 nm. Such solvent polar-dependent responses we do speculate, competition of the hydrogen bonding sites and changes of protonation/deprotonation [33].

To maximize the available information and enhance the recognition ability for organic small molecules, we proposed a dual-readout orthogonal identification scheme. This orthogonal identification strategy was demonstrated relating to both the fluorescence intensity at 455 nm (F455) and fluorescence intensity at 557 nm (F557) of S-CMP. Since the information of organic molecules can be carried by F455 and F557, each of the organic molecules corresponds to one unique 2D readout (F455, F557), which are distributed in the decoded map (Fig. 7C). These readouts are well separated in the decoded map, confirming in the decoded map, suggesting the potential for S-CMP as chemosensors to precisely differentiate these organic solvents through a dual-readout process. In comparison with the reported sensor arrays for organic contaminants recognition (Table S1), this work has advantages such as more kinds of targets and simpler data statistics method.

In summary, we have synthesized a highly water-soluble S-CMP, which can be employed to construct a multi-analyte optical sensor array for high-throughput discrimination of numerous metal ions. Using cross-reactivity discrimination analysis, S-CMP is capable of differentiate twelve metal ions in aqueous solution, suggesting the potential of the sensor array as a “chemical nose”. In addition, S-CMP reported here undergoes obvious differences in fluorescence intensities and colors when exposed to common organic solvents of different polarity. Eleven organic small molecules can be clearly differentiated by S-CMP based on a dual-readout orthogonal recognition scheme. In view of the advantages of simple design and high selectivity, this S-CMP shows great promise as a fluorescent sensor for environmental pollutants monitoring.
Ruru Gao: Luminescence properties evaluation
Wei Zhao: Preparation of CMP and S-CMP
Qian Qiu: Characterization of CMP and S-CMP
Aming Xie: Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision,
Siyao Cheng: BET analysis
Yingzhi Jiao: SEM and TEM Characterization.
Xihao Pan: XPS analysis
Wei Dong: Resources, Review & Editing, Data Curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702161 and 51573078) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 30919011219).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128448.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semicondutor quantum dots-based metal ion probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wu, T. Zhao, S. Wang, X. Hou, Semicondutor quantum dots-based metal ion probes, Nanoscale 6(2014) 43-64.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on various electrochemical techniques for heavy metal ions detection with different sensing platforms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Bansod, T. Kumar, R. Thakur, S. Rana, I. Singh, A review on various electrochemical techniques for heavy metal ions detection with different sensing platforms, Biosens. Bioelectron. 94 (2017) 443-455.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Porous organic/inorganic hybrid one-dimensional photonic crystals for rapid visual detection of organic solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Kou, S. Zhang, J. L. Lutkenhaus, L. Wang, B. Tang, W. Ma, Porous organic/inorganic hybrid one-dimensional photonic crystals for rapid visual detection of organic solvents, J. Mater. Chem. C 6 (2018) 2704-2711.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ln3+ post-functionalized metal–organic frameworks for color tunable emission and highly sensitive sensing of toxic anions and small molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. N. Hao, B. Yan, Ln3+ post-functionalized metal–organic frameworks for color tunable emission and highly sensitive sensing of toxic anions and small molecules, New J. Chem. 40 (2016) 4654-4661.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A protective layer approach to solvatochromic sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lee, H. T. Chang, H. An, S. Ahn, J. Shim, J. M. Kim, A protective layer approach to solvatochromic sensors, Nat. Commun. 4 (2013) 2461.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvatochromic behavior of a nanotubular metal− organic framework for sensing small molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Z. Lu, R. Zhang, Y. Z. Li, Z. J. Guo, H. G. Zheng, Solvatochromic behavior of a nanotubular metal− organic framework for sensing small molecules, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 133 (2011) 4172-4174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasound triggered gelation approach to selectively solvatochromic sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Feng, Z. Wang, X. Yu, H. Lan, J. Ren, L. Geng, T. Yi, An ultrasound triggered gelation approach to selectively solvatochromic sensors, Sens. Actuators, B 243 (2017) 1020-1026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triboelectrification‐enabled self‐powered detection and removal of heavy metal ions in wastewater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, J. Chen, H. Guo, X. Fan, Z. Wen, M. H. Yeh, C. Yu, X. Cao, Z. L. Wang, Triboelectrification‐Enabled Self‐Powered Detection and Removal of Heavy Metal Ions in Wastewater, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 2983-2991.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A DNA‐Based label‐free artificial tongue for pattern recognition of metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Qiu, F. Pu, X. Ran, J. Ren, X. Qu, A DNA‐Based Label‐Free Artificial Tongue for Pattern Recognition of Metal Ions, Chem. Eur. J. 23 (2017) 9258-9261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA modified Fe3O4@Au magnetic nanoparticles as selective probes for simultaneous detection of heavy metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Miao, Y. Tang, L. Wang, DNA modified Fe3O4@Au magnetic nanoparticles as selective probes for simultaneous detection of heavy metal ions, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 9 (2017) 3940-3947.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance colorimetric detection of Hg2+ based on triangular silver nanoprisms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Chen, Y. Zhang, H. Liu, X. Wu, Y. Li, L. Miao, Z. Shen, A. Wu, High-performance colorimetric detection of Hg2+ based on triangular silver nanoprisms, ACS Sensors 1 (2016) 521-527.

[5]G. Zuo, A. Xie, * J. Li, T. Su, X. Pan, W. Dong, Large Emission Red-Shift of Carbon Dots by Fluorine Doping and Their Applications for Red Cell Imaging and Sensitive Intracellular Ag+ Detection, J. Phys. Chem. C, 2017, 121, 26558-26565.
[6]J. Li, Y. Jiao, L. Feng, Y. Zhong, G. Zuo, A. Xie , W. Dong, Highly N,P-doped carbon dots: Rational design, photoluminescence and cellular imaging, Microchim. Acta, 2017, 184, 2933-2940.

[7] A. Xie , F. Wu*, W. Jiang, K. Zhang, M. Sun, M. Wang, Chiral induced synthesis of helical polypyrrole (PPy) nano-structures: a lightweight and high-performance material against electromagnetic pollution, J. Mater. Chem. C, 2017, 5, 2175-2181.

[8]Y. Jiao, F. Wu*, K. Zhang, M. Sun, A. Xie* and W. Dong, Ultra-broad polypyrrole (PPy) nano-ribbons seeded by racemic surfactants aggregates and their high-performance electromagnetic radiation elimination, Nanotechnol., 2017, 28, 315701.

[9] A. Xie ,* K. Zhang, F. Wu,* N. Wang, Y. Wang, M. Wang,* Polydopamine nanofilms as visible light-harvesting interfaces for palladium nanocrystal catalyzed coupling reactions, Catal. Sci. Technol., 2016, 6, 1764-1771.

[10] A. Xie , * F. Wu,* M. Sun, X. Dai, Z. Xu, Y. Qiu, Y. Wang,* M. Wang, Self-assembled ultralight three-dimensional polypyrrole aerogel for effective electromagnetic absorption, Appl. Phys. Lett., 2015, 106, 222902.
[11] A. Xie , M. Cao, Y. Liu, L. Feng, X. Hu, W. Dong, The Synthesis of Tetrazoles in Nanometer Aqueous Micelles at Room Temperature, Eur. J. Org. Chem., 2014, 436-441.
[12] A. Xie , X. Zhou, L. Feng, X. Hu, W. Dong, The oxidation of alcohols with O-iodoxybenzoic acid (IBX) in aqueous nanomicelles at room temperature, Tetrahedron, 2014, 70, 3514-3519.
[13]L. Feng, A. Xie , X. Hu, Y. Liu, J. Zhang, S. Li, W. Dong, A releasable disulfide carbonate linker for polyethyleneimine (PEI)-based gene vectors, New J. Chem., 2014, 38, 5207-5214.

[14]L. Feng, H. Yu, Y. Liu, X. Hu, J. Li, A. Xie , W. Dong, Construction of efficacious hepatoma-targeted nanomicelles non-covalently functionalized with galactose for drug delivery, Polym. Chem., 2014, 5, 7121-7130.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Aming Xie, an associate professor at School of Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210,094, P. R. China, Phone: +86−15250990521, E-mail: xieaming@njust.edu.cn; aminghugang@126.com, ORCID: 0000−0001-5381−0689, ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Aming_Xie?ev = prf_highl. My current research focuses on Functional polymers including conductive organic polymers, metal-organic coordination polymers, and conjugate microporous polymers (CMPs) for applications.
